citation: Goddard, J. (2020). Public libraries respond to the COVID-19 pandemic, creating a new service model. Information Technology and Libraries, 39(4), 1-4. https://doi.org/10.6017/ital.v39i4.12847
summary:
Goddard's short editorial-board essay describes how public libraries responded to the COVID-19 pandemic by re-writing service rules and creating what the author calls a new service model. The article is not a study of games, play, or game-based programming. Instead, it surveys public-library adaptations during shutdown and reopening: electronic resources, virtual community engagement, real-time reference, contactless curbside service, broadband support, online learning resources, and advocacy for emergency library funding.

The opening claim is that public libraries demonstrated community value during the pandemic by helping patrons resume ordinary life, learn, grow, and stay entertained through resources available by library card. Goddard emphasizes that librarians maintained this level of service by improvising under crisis conditions. Once buildings closed, libraries promoted ebooks and other digital platforms. OverDrive usage rose sharply, new users increased, young adult and juvenile ebook readership grew, and services such as Brainfuse, Miss Humblebee's Academy, Gale Courses, and Gale Presents: Udemy saw increased use as children, families, and adults sought school support and skill development.

The article's infrastructure section matters most for general library-service context. Goddard notes that public libraries supported patrons without sufficient broadband by leaving Wi-Fi available outside buildings, strengthening wireless signals, hosting drive-up Wi-Fi hotspot locations, partnering with schools, and lending hotspots. This digital-equity frame is relevant to GBLS only indirectly: many virtual, hybrid, streaming, Discord-based, or online-game services depend on the same connectivity, device access, and staff-support conditions. However, the article does not discuss game access, game collections, online game communities, or game-program design.

The community-engagement section describes a rapid shift to virtual programming through Facebook Live and Stories, Discord, Instagram, YouTube, Zoom, and GoToMeeting. San Jose Public Library, for example, moved story time and reading programs online, created Spring Into Reading, provided a weekly recorded phone story time, and hosted more than 2,000 virtual events after the March 2020 lockdown. Oceanside Library in New York moved quickly from closure to multiple daily online programs, including story times, book chats, and book clubs, and later opened programs to patrons from outside the state through library partnerships. Goddard writes that patrons were exercising, listening to concerts, taking virtual vacations, learning skills, cooking, playing games, and reducing stress through lockdown programming.

For the larger GBLS project, this source is useful as a reminder that library play and game services are embedded in broader service-model shifts, especially the movement toward hybrid, virtual, and contactless delivery. The mention of Discord and patrons "playing games" points toward possible pandemic-era game programming, but Goddard does not develop that point. There are no program descriptions, patron data, design recommendations, game examples, collection practices, facilitation methods, learning outcomes, or analysis of game-based service. The article is therefore too general to support a substantive GBLS claim.

The concluding section argues that the pandemic-era service model should be preserved as part of the "new normal." Goddard recommends that community programs remain both physical and virtual, physical media and books be delivered both at the desk and through contactless service, and reference continue both in person and through chat. For GBLS, this is a broad service-design backdrop rather than direct literature: future game-based library services may likewise need hybrid access models, digital-equity planning, and online facilitation capacity, but those implications must be drawn from other, more game-specific sources.

Suggested review placement: No direct addition to `review.md`. This source should be filed in `Explicitly Excluded Literature` because it is about public-library pandemic service adaptation generally and mentions games only in passing as one activity among many virtual programs. Proposed review text: None.

# Wyatt, D., & Leorke, D. (2024). Playing in the "third place": How games and play are transforming public libraries. *Space and Culture, 27*(2), 254-273. https://doi.org/10.1177/12063312231223118

Unique identifier: `wyattPlayingThirdPlace2024`; Zotero item key `EU8B37X2`; attachment key `DUYSLXMW`.

Wyatt and Leorke (2024) examine games and play as part of the spatial, temporal, and civic transformation of public libraries. Drawing on observations of library spaces and interviews with 27 library staff and managers in Australia, Finland, and Singapore, with additional examples from the United States and elsewhere, the article argues that play is not merely an added program or collection format. Games and play are reshaping library architecture, furnishings, daily rhythms, atmospheres, acoustics, boundaries, and relations to urban policy.

The article begins by challenging a simple "third place" account of libraries. Library studies often describe the public library as a place between home and work, a voluntary civic space for informal gathering, learning, and sociality. Wyatt and Leorke argue that this language remains useful but also obscures contemporary changes. Libraries are now connected to digital urbanism, creative-city policy, smart-city agendas, entrepreneurial citizenship, co-working, maker culture, digital literacy, and redevelopment projects. In that context, play can support humanistic public life, but it can also be enlisted for instrumental goals such as attracting users, producing flexible workers, supporting creative industries, and demonstrating civic innovation.

The historical section is especially useful for GBLS because it links contemporary gaming to older library debates. Games, toys, puzzles, billiards, children's rooms, and playful materials have appeared in libraries since the nineteenth century, often justified as ways to attract new users, educate youth, provide moral guidance, and offer controlled alternatives to more suspect public leisure. The authors show that contemporary objections to gaming, noise, disruption, frivolity, violence, and replacement of books are not new. They recur whenever libraries expand beyond quiet reading and print-centered seriousness.

The Finnish cases make the argument concrete. Helsinki's Oodi Central Library includes a large digital and analog game collection, a PC Gaming Room, console rooms, VR games, retro/accessible arcade spaces, tabletop rooms, role-playing clubs, and game-literacy sessions. These are not peripheral add-ons: they change how visitors move through the building, where spectators gather, how gameplay becomes visible, how rooms are scheduled, and when the library is busy. Other examples show gaming zones near shelves and makerspaces, dedicated Minecraft rooms, after-school gaming peaks, evening board-game programs, and extended opening hours tied to play.

Wyatt and Leorke also discuss children and teen zones, playful architecture, and library-city boundaries. Singaporean libraries include immersive children's rooms, maker spaces, toys, augmented-reality books, costumes, and interactive walls; Melbourne and Singapore examples include castles, tree houses, animal seating, storytelling areas, and playful learning environments. New library buildings use colorful interiors, reading nooks, responsive installations, playful facades, outdoor play areas, and transparent connections to plazas, parks, playgrounds, waterfronts, and cultural precincts. In this sense, play spills beyond designated programs and becomes part of how libraries perform publicness.

For the larger GBLS project, the article provides a needed conceptual correction. It helps move the review beyond program lists by showing that game-based library services are spatial and institutional. A game night, a gaming room, a VR station, a Minecraft club, a makerspace, or a children's play area changes sound, flow, surveillance, privacy, dwell time, staff explanation, public expectations, and the boundary between the library and the city. GBLS planning therefore has to consider architecture, acoustics, scheduling, furniture, spectatorship, children and teen zones, safety, public criticism, and policy narratives about innovation and creativity.

The article's limits are also clear. It is qualitative, interpretive, and focused largely on well-resourced public-library examples, especially in Finland, Australia, and Singapore. It does not measure program outcomes, attendance, learning, belonging, or patron satisfaction. Its value is not causal evidence but analytic framing: it shows why libraries' increasing investment in play is socially consequential and why the public library as "third place" is both strengthened and unsettled by games.

Suggested review placement: Add to `# Conceptual Terrain: Games, Play, and Library Service` > `## Games, Gamification, and Play`, after the Downie and Proulx (2022) paragraph. Proposed text: "Wyatt and Leorke (2024) widen the review's concept of play by arguing that public-library games are not only artifacts, events, or gamified mechanics. In observations and 27 staff interviews across Australia, Finland, and Singapore, they show that play also appears in architecture, furnishings, acoustics, daily rhythms, children's zones, gaming rooms, makerspaces, outdoor connections, and urban-development narratives, making the library itself more playful while complicating the familiar 'third place' frame."

Suggested review placement: Add to `# Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service` > `## Community, Belonging, and the Public Commons`, after the Khademi Zare, Varnaseri, and Bayati (2024) paragraph. Proposed text: "Wyatt and Leorke (2024) make the public-library commons spatial: gaming rooms, console rooms, VR play, tabletop spaces, Minecraft areas, children's play zones, playful interiors, and porous links to plazas and parks change how patrons gather, watch, move, make noise, dwell, and understand who the library is for. Their cases show that GBLS can transform the atmosphere and civic meaning of the library, not just its program calendar."

Suggested review placement: Add to `# Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services` > `## Plan for Operations and Sustainability`, after the Swiatek and Gorsse (2016) paragraph. Proposed text: "Wyatt and Leorke (2024) show that operational planning for GBLS includes spatial and atmospheric governance. Libraries that add gaming zones, transparent console rooms, VR, Minecraft, evening board games, playful children's areas, or outdoor play links must manage noise, visibility, privacy, spectatorship, school-hour temptation, dwell time, public criticism, and the tension between welcoming play and preserving quiet, safe, multipurpose civic space."
sourceText:
EDITORIAL BOARD THOUGHTS
Public Libraries Respond to the COVID-19 Pandemic, Creating a New Service Model
Jon Goddard
During the COVID-19 pandemic, public libraries have demonstrated, in many ways, their value to their communities. They have enabled their patrons to not only resume their lives, but to help them learn and grow. Additionally, electronic resources offered to patrons through their library card have allowed people to be educated and entertained.
The credit must go to the librarians, who initially fueled, and have maintained this level of service by re-writing the rules—creating a new service model.
Once libraries closed, librarians promoted ebooks and other important platforms available to patrons with their library cards. The result: The checkout of ebooks, and the use of these platforms rose, exponentially.
Community engagement became completely virtual with librarians, and those who provide library programs to the public, providing services on platforms that they may or may not have heard of, such as Zoom and Discord.
As libraries re-opened, many offered real-time reference services, as well as seamless and contactless curbside service, providing a sense of control and continuity amongst the chaos.
EXPONENTIAL INCREASES IN ELECTRONIC RESOURCE USAGE
Overdrive, which is currently used by nearly 90% of public libraries in the United States to manage both ebook and audiobook collections, saw an exponential increase in its usage. Since the lockdown began in mid-March, the daily average for ebook checkouts have been consistently 52% above pre-COVID periods. Additionally, new users to the platform have been consistently double and triple 2019 highs.1
Library staff have been helping readers during this time to ensure they obtain access with their devices. In Suffolk County, New York, where new patron registration to Overdrive is up 72% from last year (as of August 2020), there has been no shortage of requests for help.2
With kids being home from school and learning virtually, it is no surprise that ebook readership skyrocketed amongst YA and Juvenile readers with an 87% increase from last year.3 To help them with their homework and studies, families turned to online tutoring. In Suffolk County, New York, the usage of the Brainfuse online live tutoring service has been consistently up by nearly 50% during the school closures.4 Gale, a Cengage company, which offers Miss Humblebee's Academy, a virtual learning program for preschoolers, saw its user sessions increase by 100% from the previous year.5
Jon Goddard (jgoddard@northshorepubliclibrary.org) is a Librarian at the North Shore Public Library, and a member of the ITAL Editorial Board. © 2020.
INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND LIBRARIES | DECEMBER 2020 https://doi.org/10.6017/ital.v39i4.12847

INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND LIBRARIES

DECEMBER 2020

Adults, also eager to learn new skills, took to online courses as well. Gale Courses saw a 50% increase in enrollments from March-July from the previous year. Likewise, Gale Presents: Udemy, which offers on-demand video courses, saw just over 21,000 course enrollments from MarchJune.6
To help those who did not have sufficient broadband Wifi to use these necessary resources and platforms, many libraries left their Wifi on even when the building was closed to allow access to those in the vicinity of the building. In addition, many libraries purchased Wifi hotspots to lend to their patrons.
According to Pew Research, approximately 25% of households do not have a broadband internet connection at home.7 While public libraries cannot provide the only local solution to this gap, here are other steps libraries have been taking during the shutdown:
• Strengthening wireless signals so people can access wireless from outside library buildings. • Hosting drive-up Wifi hotspot locations. • Partnering with schools to obtain and distribute Wifi hotspots to families in need.
COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT - VIRTUALLY
Community engagement has been vital since the COVID-19 lockdown. Both librarians and those who provide library programs to the public had to quickly adjust to the virtual world in which we were suddenly living.
Using a mixture of social media platforms, including Facebook Live and Stories, Discord, Instagram, YouTube, Zoom, and GoToMeeting, librarians flocked to the internet, providing a wide range of programming.
Even those libraries that did not previously have any virtual programs managed to very quickly provide quality programs to their patrons.
Virtual programming was not available at the San José Public Library (SJPL) prior to the shutdown. Librarians quickly started to move programs online, including story time, and created a program called Spring Into Reading, similar to the summer reading program, to continue to encourage families to read together. They also started a weekly recorded story time, so patrons could call the library and use their phones to hear a story. To date, SJPL has hosted over 2,000 virtual events since the lockdown began on March 17th.8
Some libraries, like the Oceanside Library in New York, were offering virtual programs before the pandemic. When the library closed on March 13, the team started planning to move completely virtual. Two days later, the library was offering four programs a day, including story times, book chats, and book clubs. By the end of the week, they were offering eight programs a day.9 In April, May, and June, they found book discussions and story times were the most popular programs. They then started to open their programs to people from out of state, partnering with other libraries. The result? Program attendance has increased and several Zoom meeting rooms have been maxed out.10
Through the lockdown, library patrons have been exercising, listening to concerts, taking virtual vacations, learning new skills, cooking, playing games, and reducing stress. This incredible

PUBLIC LIBRARIES RESPOND TO THE COVID-19 PANDEMIC | GODDARD

2

INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND LIBRARIES

DECEMBER 2020

adaption was only possible due to library worker’s quick thinking and a never-ending determination to help.
DELIVERING INFORMATION AND MATERIALS WITH A NEW SERVICE MODEL
At the San Jose Public Library (SJPL), which has over 500,000 library members, library staff had to quickly shift to a new online reality just after the shutdown.
To help patrons get the most from their electronic resources, SJPL used LibAnswers to post FAQs and email responses to their issues and questions. When a librarian was available, patrons could use LibChat to ask questions in real-time.
Because no one was in the library buildings to answer phones, LibAnswers and LibChat became the only way the public could communicate with staff. Chat reference conversations increased by nearly 400%—from approximately 40 chat sessions per day to 160 per day. The chat service was also made available in Spanish, Vietnamese, and Chinese.
When the library implemented its Express Pickup service, SJPL utilized the Spaces functionality in LibCal to allow patrons to create pickup appointments. When patrons arrived at the library for their appointment, the SMS functionality in LibAnswers allowed patrons to text staff upon arrival.
Through the City of San José’s SJ Access initiative, which aims to help bridge the digital divide in the city, SJPL worked closely with other city departments, and the Santa Clara County Office of Education, to purchase approximately 16,000 high-speed AT&T hotspots for students and the public.11
Working Towards the New Normal
The American Library Association (ALA) is committed to advocate strongly for libraries on several different fronts.
Thanks to thousands of advocate communications with Congress, libraries secured $50 million for the Institute of Museum and Library Services (IMLS) in the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security (CARES) Act. This enabled libraries and museums to apply for grants during this time of need.12
In addition, the ALA is currently advocating for the passage of the Library Stabilization Fund Act (S.4181 / H.R.7486) to allow libraries to retain staff, maintain services, and safely keep communities connected and informed. The legislation calls for $2 billion in emergency recovery funding for America's libraries through the Institute of Museum and Library Services (IMLS).13
While the ALA is rightly advocating for these emergency funds, public librarians and administrators should take advantage of this time to strategically review what has been put into place to react to the COVID-19 pandemic, and plan for the long term. While it is true that libraries are physical spaces, they are also technology-driven services for learning and connections for all ages. Additionally, they have shown that due to this new service model, access has exponentially expanded to new patrons, showing tremendous value when it comes to education and engagement.

PUBLIC LIBRARIES RESPOND TO THE COVID-19 PANDEMIC | GODDARD

3

INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND LIBRARIES

DECEMBER 2020

This new service model should be preserved. Programs that engage our communities should be both physical and virtual. Physical media and books should be provided both at the circulation desk and through a contactless service. Reference services should be provided both at the reference desk, and through chat reference services. This must be our new normal.
ENDNOTES
1 David Burleigh, Director, Brand Marketing & Communication at Overdrive, phone conversation with author, October 9, 2020.
2 Maureen McDonald, Special Projects Supervisor at the Suffolk Cooperative Library System, phone conversation, September 14, 2020.
3 Burleigh.
4 McDonald.
5 Kayla Siefker, Head of Media & Public Relations at Gale, a Cengage Company, Brian Risse, VP of Sales – Public Libraries. Muna Sharif, Product Manager, Discovery & Analytics, phone conversation with author, October 16, 2020.
6 Siefker.
7 Pew Research Center, “Internet/Broadband Fact Sheet,” June 12, 2019, accessed October 13, 2020, https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/fact-sheet/internet-broadband/.
8 Laurie Willis, Web Services at SJPL, Phone conversation with author, October 14, 2020.
9 Erica Freudenberger, “Programming Through the Pandemic,” Library Journal, May 22, 2020, https://www.libraryjournal.com/?detailStory=Programming-Through-the-Pandemic-covid19.
10 Tony Iovino, Assistant Director for Community Services at the Oceanside Library, phone conversation with author, October 19, 2020.
11 Willis.
12 American Library Association, “Advocacy & Policy,” accessed October 15, 2020, http://www.ala.org/tools/covid/advocacy-policy.
13 Ibid.

PUBLIC LIBRARIES RESPOND TO THE COVID-19 PANDEMIC | GODDARD

4

Both librarians and those who provide library programs to the public had to quickly adjust to the virtual world in which we were suddenly living.
Some libraries, like the Oceanside Library in New York, were offering virtual programs before the pandemic.

Library_Context.cultural_heritage_instit.quote: Overdrive, which is currently used by nearly 90% of public libraries in the United States to manage both ebook and audiobook collections, saw an exponential increase in its usage.
Through the lockdown, library patrons have been exercising, listening to concerts, taking virtual vacations, learning new skills, cooking, playing games, and reducing stress.

Thanks to thousands of advocate communications with Congress, libraries secured $50 million for the Institute of Museum and Library Services (IMLS) in the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security (CARES) Act.
